metadata.title:
REAL ESTATE MEN SW0OP


DOWN ON PUBLIC MARKET

pages.raw_text:
be taken up and dissected, He cited
the Seattle plan whereby farmers
are not charged cent and the only
expense the city is not involved
financially to any degree.
Commissioner O'Brien went fur
Their into the Seattle plan, explain
ing that the only expense there is
a policeman who permits no buying
from producers for booth supplies
until the public has had an oppor-
tunity to trade direct from the
wagons.


The whole matter was finally left
to committee composed of Gray,
Estep and Pratt to get in shape
for future presentation. Thomp-
sons project has a small lobby
working for it and the other inter-
ests are equally busy. Unless the
council exhibits more actual in
terest in the market than has here
tofore been evident thte market
will be dickered out of existence.
The growers association is natural
ly against it, and it will get plenty
Of help from disappointed realty
dealers.


Mayor Moore is set against any
dealing for the purchase of site
or other elaborate arrangements
in which it will be an easy matter
to lose the project. He wants a
free market and is positive 1t can
be established in months time
without noticeable expense to the
city, simply by setting aside por
tons of an unused street and erect
ing the necessary sheds. Farmers
could then come in and the public
could go to the market and buy.
Commissioner O'Brien is of the
same opinion.


In the meantime the council
seems disposed to daily with all
the complicated schemes enterpris-
ing real estate men can present to
unload property on the city at
time when the administration is
facing situation that demands the
most careful financiering.
